docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Testing emojis added to this site

Welcome to the Getting Started Guide. Whether you're new to our platform or just need a refresher, this guide will walk you through the steps to get up and running quickly.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Pellentesque auctor tincidunt leo, vel dapibus est. Sed sed lectus vitae felis malesuada lacinia. Integer feugiat nisl ut velit tempor, in ultricies orci elementum. Sed ac posuere neque. Integer euismod velit ut elit fermentum, ac malesuada purus pretium. Suspendisse potenti.

Our platform is a powerful tool designed to help you achieve X, Y, and Z. It comes with a range of features to facilitate A, B, and C. This guide will ensure you're up and running in no time.

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text: Before you get started, make sure your system meets the following requirements:

docs.title: Hardware:
docs.text:
• CPU: Dual-core processor or higher
  • RAM: 4GB minimum (8GB recommended)
  • Disk Space: 1GB of free space for the base installation

docs.title: Software:
docs.text:
• Operating System: macOS 10.15+, Windows 10+, or a Linux-based distribution (Ubuntu 20.04 or newer recommended)
  • Dependencies:
  • Node.js (version 14 or newer)
  • Git (for version control)
  • Python (version 3.6 or higher)

If your system doesn't meet these requirements, you may experience performance issues.

docs.title: Installation Instructions
docs.text: Once you've ensured your system meets the requirements, follow the installation steps for your operating system.

docs.title: For macOS:
docs.text:
1. Open your terminal.
  2. Install using Homebrew:

```bash brew install our-software

docs.title: Primary Button
docs.text:
This component highlights the most important actions of the page. Should be used once per page and often placed in a prominent location.

Return to Team Repository

docs.title: Primary Button with Icon
docs.text: Return to Team Repository

docs.title: Secondary Buttons
docs.text:
Secondary buttons represent less critical actions that are often alternatives or complementary to the primary action. Can be placed near the primary button, but are not as visually prominen

Back to Home

docs.title: Python Example
docs.text:
Tip

You can easily include code snippets within your documentation using fenced code blocks.

def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("World")

docs.title: Diagrams
docs.text: Testing emojis added to this site

docs.title: Flowcharts
docs.text:
Flowcharts are diagrams that represent workflows or processes

graph LR
  A[Start] --> B{Failure?};
  B -->|Yes| C[Investigate...];
  C --> D[Debug];
  D --> B;
  B ---->|No| E[Success!];

docs.title: Sequence Diagrams
docs.text:
Sequence diagrams describe a specific scenario as sequential interactions between multiple objects

sequenceDiagram
  autonumber
  Server->>Terminal: Send request
  loop Health
      Terminal->>Terminal: Check for health
  end
  Note right of Terminal: System online
  Terminal-->>Server: Everything is OK
  Terminal->>Database: Request customer data
  Database-->>Terminal: Customer data
